notes: Dominant workgroup and departmental server OS; limitations in enterprise stability/scalability.
Dominant NT server hardware; Pentium II Xeon for >4-way SMP arriving mid-1998.
64-bit architecture co-developed with HP; scheduled late 1999; Aberdeen predicts 5-year IA-32 to IA-64 transition.
~2% NT server market; higher performance than Intel for transaction workloads; reaching 660 MHz by late 1998.
Introduced fall 1997; two-node failover only; not full HA; load balancing not yet provided.
Key NT server differentiator; 4-way dominant; 8-way arriving 1998; proprietary vs. Intel-standard debate.
Planned for NT 5.0; key for enterprise scalability and directory services.
Intel-backed I/O architecture for NT servers; improving I/O subsystem bandwidth.
Industry standard clustering interconnect; Intel-backed; complement to Tandem ServerNet.
Dominant enterprise server OS; Aberdeen projects global Unix app server market at $40B by 2000 vs NT $9B.
Key NT server application; mail and messaging.
Storage interconnect; Dell OEMing Data General Fibre Channel storage.
Evolving NT systems management standards offering hope for NT robustness improvements.
